topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my screen, a jarring intrusion into the smooth rhythm of my afternoon. A profile picture loaded: a familiar, sneering face I hadn't seen in fifteen years. My jaw tightened almost imperceptibly as I clicked on the request. It was Mark. The kid who used to orchestrate elaborate schemes just to make me feel small. A slow, almost amused smirk touched my lips. I scrolled through the profile, cataloging the superficial updates, before tapping the 'Accept' button with deliberate slowness. A small, almost silent victory bloomed in my chest, a quiet affirmation that the past held no power over the present. I leaned back in my chair, the leather creaking softly under my weight, and waited.

[story 2]

When the ping arrived, I didn't even look up from the complex architectural plans spread across my drafting table. My pen paused mid-stroke, hovering over a crucial structural beam. A flicker of something—not surprise, but a distinct sense of knowing—crossed my features. It was Mark. The memory of his taunts, the way his voice used to echo in the hallway, was sharp, but today, it felt distant, like a poorly focused photograph. I picked up my coffee mug, the ceramic warm against my palms, and took a slow, measured sip. I typed a brief, noncommittal reply, my fingers moving with practiced ease. I didn't rush the response; every word I chose was calibrated, precise, leaving no room for weakness.

[story 3]

The phone buzzed. I glanced at it, and the familiar name flashed across the screen. My breath hitched for a fraction of a second, a physical reaction I quickly suppressed. It was him. The boy who thrived on undermining me. Instead of feeling the usual knot of anxiety, a strange sense of detachment settled over me. I leaned my head against the cool glass of the window, watching the city lights blur below. I didn't immediately answer. I let the silence stretch, allowing the anticipation to build, a taut wire humming just beneath the surface of my composure. When I finally typed, my tone was cool, almost clinical, a deliberate display of unruffled control.
